| Largest loss             = {{fb|URU}} 9–0 {{fb-rt|BOL}} <br /> ([[Lima]], [[Peru]]; 6 November 1927) <br /> {{fb|BRA|1889}} 10–1 {{fb-rt|BOL}} <br /> ([[São Paulo]], [[Brazil]]; 10 April 1949)
| World cup apps           = 3
| World cup first          = 1930
| World cup best           = Group stage ([[1930 FIFA World Cup|1930]], [[1950 FIFA World Cup|1950]], [[1994 FIFA World Cup|1994]])
| Regional name            = [[Copa América]]
| Regional cup apps        = 29
| Regional cup first       = [[1926 South American Championship|1926]]
| Regional cup best        = '''Champions''' ([[1963 South American Championship|1963]])
| Confederations cup apps  = 1
| Confederations cup first = [[1999 FIFA Confederations Cup|1999]]
| Confederations cup best  = Group stage ([[1999 FIFA Confederations Cup|1999]])
| medaltemplates           =
{{MedalCompetition|[[Copa América]]}}
{{MedalGold|[[1963 South American Championship|1963 Bolivia]]|[[1963 South American Championship squads#Bolivia|Team]]}}
{{MedalSilver|[[1997 Copa América|1997 Bolivia]]|[[1997 Copa América squads#Bolivia|Team]]}}
|World cup last=1994}}

The '''Bolivia national football team''' ({{Langx|es|Selección de fútbol de Bolivia}}), nicknamed '''''La Verde''''', has represented [[Bolivia]] in men's international [[Association football|football]] since 1926. Organized by the [[Bolivian Football Federation|Federación Boliviana de Fútbol]] ({{Lit|Bolivian Football Federation}}),{{efn-ua|The acronym FBF comes from the organization's Spanish name, ''Federación Boliviana de Fútbol''.}} it is one of the ten members of [[FIFA]]'s South American Football Confederation ([[CONMEBOL]]).

After playing in the [[1930 FIFA World Cup|1930]] and [[1950 FIFA World Cup|1950]] [[FIFA World Cup|World Cups]], they have qualified just once, in [[1994 FIFA World Cup|1994]], where they were eliminated in the group stage. Bolivia have never advanced past the first round of any World Cup, and have only scored one goal, in 1994. Despite their World Cup performances, Bolivia won the [[Copa América]] at home in [[1963 South American Championship|1963]], and finished runners-up in [[1997 Copa América|1997]], which they also hosted. At the [[2015 Copa América]] in Chile, they advanced to the quarter-finals for the first time since 1997, after defeating [[Ecuador national football team|Ecuador]] 3–2. This also ended a winless streak in the Copa América, with their last victory being on 28 June 1997, when they defeated [[Mexico national football team|Mexico]] 1–0 in the semi-finals.<ref>{{cite web|date=15 June 2015|title=Ecuador 2−3 Bolivia|url=http://futbol.univision.com/copa-america/article/2015-06-15/ecuador-2-bolivia-3-cronica?ftloc=channel566:wcmWidgetUimStage&ftpos=channel566:wcmWidgetUimStage:1&hootPostID=45f58e8c40d8360c7e909014610475b7#axzz3dBb8CynY|access-date=16 June 2015|website=Univision futbol|archive-date=2 April 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230402141832/https://www.tudn.com/futbol#axzz3dBb8CynY|url-status=live}}</ref>

==History==
[[File:Bolivia 1930 2AB.jpg|thumb|left|alt=Photo of twelve men, seven standing and five crouching, inside a stadium|Bolivia national team at the [[1930 FIFA World Cup]] before their match against Yugoslavia]]
Bolivia debuted in international football in 1926, one year after the [[Bolivian Football Federation]] was founded, and joined FIFA that same year. As participants at the [[1926 South American Championship]] in [[Chile]], Bolivia played their first match against [[Chile national football team|the hosts]] on 12 October 1926, and even ended up scoring first against them, but wound up being defeated by the Chileans 7–1. Bolivia also lost their following three matches: 0–5 against [[Argentina national football team|Argentina]], 1–6 against [[Paraguay national football team|Paraguay]] and 0–6 against [[Uruguay national football team|Uruguay]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://historiadelfutbolboliviano.com/2011/02/11/historia-de-nuestro-futbol-capitulo-2-nacen-la-fbf-y-la-seleccion-1925-1926/|title=Historia de Nuestro Fútbol, Capítulo 2. Nacen la FBF y la Selección 1925-1926|website=Historia del Fútbol Boliviano|date=11 February 2011|access-date=25 May 2020|language=es|archive-date=27 September 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200927043816/https://historiadelfutbolboliviano.com/2011/02/11/historia-de-nuestro-futbol-capitulo-2-nacen-la-fbf-y-la-seleccion-1925-1926/|url-status=live}}</ref>

In 1930, Bolivia was one of the teams invited to the inaugural edition of the [[FIFA World Cup|World Cup]], held in [[Uruguay]]. Drawn in [[1930 FIFA World Cup Group 2|Group 2]] of the [[1930 FIFA World Cup|1930 World Cup]], Bolivia lost both its games 4–0, first against [[Yugoslavia national football team|Yugoslavia]] at the [[Estadio Parque Central]], then [[Brazil national football team|Brazil]] in the [[Estadio Centenario]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://historiadelfutbolboliviano.com/2014/06/12/bolivia-en-la-copa-del-mundo-capitulo-1-uruguay-1930/|title=Bolivia en la Copa del Mundo, Capítulo 1. Uruguay 1930|website=Historia del Fútbol Boliviano|date=12 June 2014|access-date=25 May 2020|archive-date=7 August 2020|language=es|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200807030707/https://historiadelfutbolboliviano.com/2014/06/12/bolivia-en-la-copa-del-mundo-capitulo-1-uruguay-1930/|url-status=live}}</ref> The match against the Yugoslavs would be the last match against a non-South American opponent for Bolivia until 1972 {{En dash}} when they again met Yugoslavia.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesb/bol-intres.html|first=Macario|last=Reyes Padilla|title=Bolivia: International Results|website=RSSSF|access-date=22 April 2015|url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150428060143/http://www.rsssf.com/tablesb/bol-intres.html |archive-date=28 April 2015}}</ref> They returned for the [[1950 FIFA World Cup|1950 World Cup]], where Argentina's withdrawal from the qualifiers gave Bolivia an automatic berth. With three teams declining to play in [[Brazil]], Bolivia was put in a group of two along with Uruguay. The Bolivians' only game was an 8–0 defeat to Uruguay at the [[Arena Independência|Estádio Independência]] in [[Belo Horizonte]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://historiadelfutbolboliviano.com/2014/07/13/bolivia-en-la-copa-del-mundo-capitulo-2-brasil-1950/|title=Bolivia en la Copa del Mundo, Capítulo 2. Brasil 1950|website=Historia del Fútbol Boliviano|date=13 July 2014|access-date=25 May 2020|language=es|archive-date=12 August 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200812150132/https://historiadelfutbolboliviano.com/2014/07/13/bolivia-en-la-copa-del-mundo-capitulo-2-brasil-1950/|url-status=live}}</ref>

[[File:Bolivia 1963.jpg|thumb|The Bolivian squad that won its first and only Copa América title in 1963]]
Bolivia's greatest football achievement was the [[1963 South American Championship]] title, which they hosted and won after placing first out of 7 countries, including being undefeated, with five wins and one draw. The only draw for Bolivia in the tournament was a 4–4 draw against Ecuador in the opening match. They also had the advantage of being better accustomed to [[Effects of high altitude on humans|higher altitudes]].<ref name="auto">{{Cite web|url=http://www77.ca2011.com/|title=Ca2011.com|website=www77.ca2011.com|access-date=25 May 2020|archive-date=3 June 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200603011916/http://www77.ca2011.com/|url-status=dead}}</ref> In the following edition, the [[1967 South American Championship]], held in Uruguay, Bolivia finished last out of six teams, with one draw and four losses, which was far below what the public expected, as Bolivia had been the defending champion.

Afterwards, the country only started to resurge at an international level with the creation of the [[w:es:Academia Tahuichi Aguilera|Academia Tahuichi Aguilera]] in [[Santa Cruz de la Sierra]] in 1978, a football school that developed players such as [[Marco Etcheverry]], [[Erwin Sánchez]] and [[Luis Cristaldo]].

Under Spanish coach [[Xabier Azkargorta]] and featuring nine players from Tahuichi, Bolivia surprisingly became the first team to beat Brazil in the [[1994 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONMEBOL)|1994 World Cup qualifiers]] while playing them in [[La Paz]], with a 2–0 win, and qualified for the [[1994 FIFA World Cup|1994 World Cup]] by finishing second in Group B behind the Brazilians themselves, which included record 7–0 and 7–1 wins over Venezuela during their qualification campaign.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.tahuichi.com.bo/en_historia.php |title=En Historia|access-date=14 July 2014|language=es|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140715105711/http://www.tahuichi.com.bo/en_historia.php |archive-date=15 July 2014 |url-status=dead}}</ref>

Bolivia was drawn into the tournament's [[1994 FIFA World Cup Group C|Group C]], and played defending champions [[Germany national football team|Germany]] in the tournament's opening match at [[Soldier Field]]. Bolivia outplayed Germany in the first half. In the second half, [[Lothar Matthäus]] took a 40-yard run and struck Marco "El Diablo" Etcheverry with a high elbow to his jaw. Etcheverry retaliated by fouling Matthäus and was sent off. Eventually, Bolivia lost on a controversial offside goal by [[Jürgen Klinsmann]]. Following a goalless draw with [[South Korea national football team|South Korea]] at [[Foxboro Stadium]], where Bolivia was forced to play with ten men again after Cristaldo's red card, Bolivia returned to Chicago and lost 3–1 against [[Spain national football team|Spain]], with Sánchez scoring the first ever Bolivian goal in a World Cup.<ref>{{Cite web|title=1994 FIFA World Cup Technical Report|url=https://www.fifa.com/en/mm/document/afdeveloping/technicaldevp/50/08/57/wc%5f94%5ftr%5fpart3%5f270.pdf|url-status=dead|publisher=FIFA|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111220065721/http://www.fifa.com/mm/document/afdeveloping/technicaldevp/50/08/57/wc%5f94%5ftr%5fpart3%5f270.pdf|archive-date=20 December 2011|page=133}}</ref>

Following the World Cup, Bolivia participated in the [[1995 Copa América]] held in Uruguay, with [[Antonio Lopez Habas]] as manager, where they made the quarter-finals for the first time since winning the competition in 1963, with one win, one draw, and one loss. In the quarter-finals, the nation lost to hosts Uruguay 2–1. Despite the decent performance the team displayed during the tournament, Lopez Habas left his post after the [[1997 Copa América|1997 Copa America]], being replaced by [[Dušan Drašković]]. The 1997 edition was the second time Bolivia held the tournament. The team reached the [[1997 Copa América Final|final]], as had happened last time Bolivia was the host, but this time they finished runner-up to reigning world champion Brazil after losing 3–1 in [[1997 Copa América Final|the final]].<ref name="auto"/>

[[File:Ecuador-Bolivia 2015 (6).jpg|thumb|Bolivia before a match against [[Ecuador national football team|Ecuador]] during the [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONMEBOL)|2018 World Cup qualifiers]]]]
With their runner-up finish at the previous Copa America, Bolivia made their first and only [[FIFA Confederations Cup]] appearance in the [[1999 FIFA Confederations Cup|1999 edition]], this time under new Argentine manager [[Héctor Veira]]. Bolivia was placed in group A along with hosts [[Mexico national football team|Mexico]], [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]], and [[Egypt national football team|Egypt]]. Their campaign started with a 2–2 draw against Egypt. Their next match was a goalless draw against Saudi Arabia. For their last match in the group, they had to play hosts Mexico, in which Bolivia lost 0–1 with a goal from [[Francisco Palencia]]. Bolivia finished third in the group with two draws and a loss, being eliminated from the tournament in the first stage.

In the [[2015 Copa América]] in [[Chile]], under Bolivian manager [[Mauricio Soria]], Bolivia were placed in [[2015 Copa América Group A|Group A]], with [[Chile national football team|Chile]], Mexico, and [[Ecuador national football team|Ecuador]]. In their match against Mexico, Bolivia drew goalless. However, against Ecuador, Bolivia won 3–2, with goals from [[Ronald Raldes|Raldes]], [[Martin Smedberg-Dalence|Smedberg-Dalence]], and [[Marcelo Martins Moreno|Moreno]]. From this victory against Ecuador, Bolivia made it to the [[2015 Copa América#Quarter-finals|next round]], the quarter-finals, for the first time since the 1997 tournament, which they hosted.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Grupo A: Bolivia derrota 3-2 a Ecuador y acaricia los cuartos|url=http://conmebol.com/es/15062015-1911/grupo-bolivia-derrota-3-2-ecuador-y-acaricia-los-cuartos|url-status=dead|website=CONMEBOL|date=15 June 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150617182301/http://conmebol.com/es/15062015-1911/grupo-bolivia-derrota-3-2-ecuador-y-acaricia-los-cuartos|archive-date=17 June 2015}}</ref> Bolivia was defeated by [[Peru national football team|Peru]] 1–3 in the [[2015 Copa América knockout stage#Bolivia vs Peru|quarter-finals]] of the tournament, and Bolivia's only goal of the game was a [[Penalty kick (association football)|penalty]] in the last minutes of the match scored by Marcelo Moreno. In the next three Copa América editions, Bolivia performed poorly, losing all games in these tournaments.

In 2021, newly appointed president of Bolivian Football Federation, Fernando Costa Sarmiento, lamented the deteriorating condition of football in Bolivia and vowed to rebuild the country's football system. He accused the previous Presidents of mismanaging football as he aimed to reconstruct Bolivian football into a more professional manner.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://jornada.com.bo/fernando-costa-a-reconstruir-el-futbol/ |title=Fernando Costa: "A reconstruir el fútbol"|website=Jornada|date=24 May 2021 |access-date=31 March 2023|language=es|archive-date=31 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230331110802/https://jornada.com.bo/fernando-costa-a-reconstruir-el-futbol/ |url-status=live}}</ref>

==Stadium==
Bolivia played their home matches at [[Estadio Hernando Siles]], which has an altitude of {{convert|3637|m}} above sea level, making it one of the highest football stadiums in the world. Many visiting teams have protested that the altitude gives Bolivia an unfair advantage against opponents. On 27 May 2007, [[FIFA]] declared that no World Cup Qualifying matches could be played in stadiums above 8,200 feet (2,500 m) above sea level.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Anger Echoes in South America After FIFA Bans Games at Altitude|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2007/05/29/sports/29soccer.html|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|agency=[[Associated Press]]|date=29 May 2007|access-date=14 October 2021|issn=0362-4331|archive-date=17 August 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170817082740/http://www.nytimes.com/2007/05/29/sports/29soccer.html|url-status=live}}</ref> However, FIFA raised the altitude limit to 3,000 meters a month later after negative feedback against the ban, and included a special exception for La Paz, thus allowing the stadium to continue holding World Cup qualifying matches.<ref>{{Cite news|title=FIFA excludes La Paz from altitude ban|url=https://www.reuters.com/article/soccer-bolivia-fifa-idUKN2827479220070629|agency=[[Reuters]]|date=29 June 2007|access-date=14 October 2021|archive-date=29 October 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211029174050/https://www.reuters.com/article/soccer-bolivia-fifa-idUKN2827479220070629|url-status=live}}</ref> A year after the original ban, in May 2008, FIFA removed the altitude limit entirely.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Fifa suspends ban on high-altitude football|url=http://www.theguardian.com/football/2008/may/29/bolivia.bolivia|newspaper=[[The Guardian]]|date=28 May 2008|access-date=14 October 2021|archive-date=23 October 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211023231818/https://www.theguardian.com/football/2008/may/29/bolivia.bolivia|url-status=live}}</ref>
In 2024 the Bolivian Football Federation decided that from then on the home games would be played in the [[Estadio Municipal de El Alto]], that has an altitude of {{convert|4150|m}} above sea level. The official reasoning by the coach is that it was freshly renovated and that they would play where they live.<ref>{{Cite magazine|title="Wir spielen, wo wir leben": Bolivien und sein umstrittenes Stadion auf 4150 Metern|url=https://www.kicker.de/wir-spielen-wo-wir-leben-bolivien-und-sein-umstrittenes-stadion-auf-4150-metern-1057738/artikel|magazine=[[Kicker (magazine)|Kicker]]|date=8 October 2024|language=de}}</ref>

==Team image==
===Kit history===
Bolivia's first uniforms were all white. In the 1930 FIFA World Cup, before the match with Yugoslavia, Bolivia painted one of the letters in "Viva Uruguay" in each of the eleven starters' jerseys to please the local crowd. In the following game with Brazil, given the adversary also wore white, Bolivia instead borrowed Uruguay's own blue uniform to play. Bolivia again painted a message to the hosts in the [[1945 South American Championship]], with the players' jerseys reading "Viva Chile". In 1946, Bolivia changed their jersey colors to black and white stripes, like the colors of the [[Cochabamba]] region. FBF reverted to white the following year. In 1957, FBF decided to use the colors of the [[Flag of Bolivia]]. Given that red and yellow were used by many of the other South American national teams, green became the primary color, leading to the nickname "La Verde" ("The Green").<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.serieaddicted.com/article/world-cup-kits_when-bolivia-wore-uruguayan-shirts-to-ingratiate-fans-.php |title=World Cup Kits: When Bolivia wore Uruguayan shirts to ingratiate fans|website=Serie Addicted|access-date=14 July 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140715035955/http://www.serieaddicted.com/article/world-cup-kits_when-bolivia-wore-uruguayan-shirts-to-ingratiate-fans-.php |archive-date=15 July 2014 |url-status=dead}}</ref>

El rival de la Verde será un equipo del sindicato de futbolistas|trans-title=Peru vs. Bolivia? La Verde's opponent will be a team from the footballers' union|website=Brújula Noticias|date=15 December 2025|access-date=16 December 2025|language=es}}</ref>
}}

| Head coach

| Assistant coach

===Coaching history===
:Caretaker managers are listed in ''italics''.
{{Div col|colwidth=22em}}
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Jose de la Cerda]] (1926)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Jorge Valderrama]] (1927–1929)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Ulises Saucedo]] (1930–1937)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Julio Borelli]] (1938–1945)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Diógenes Lara]] (1945–1947)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Félix Deheza]] (1948–1950)
*{{flagicon|ITA}} [[Mario Pretto]] (1950–1952)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} [[César Viccino]] (1953–1958)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Vicente Arraya]] (1959)
*{{flagicon|BRA|1889}} [[Danilo Alvim]] (1960–1965)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Freddy Valda]] (1965)
*{{flagicon|GRE|old}} [[Dan Georgiadis]] (1966–1967)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Freddy Valda]] (1969)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Freddy Valda]] (1973)
*{{flagicon|FRG}} [[Rudi Gutendorf]] (1974)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Freddy Valda]] (1975)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Ramiro Blacut]] (1979–1981)
*{{flagicon|CHI}} [[Raúl Pino]] (1985)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Ramiro Blacut]] (1985–1987)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} [[Jorge Habegger]] (1988–1990)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Ramiro Blacut]] (1991–1992)
*{{flagicon|ESP}} [[Xabier Azkargorta]] (1993–1994)
*{{flagicon|ESP}} [[Antonio López Habas]] (1995–1997)
*{{flagicon|SCG}} [[Dušan Drašković]] (1997–1998)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} [[Héctor Veira]] (1998–2000)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Carlos Aragonés]] (2000–2001)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} [[Jorge Habegger]] (2001)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} {{flagicon|ARG}} [[Carlos Trucco]] (2001–2002)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} [[Dalcio Giovagnoli]] (2003)
*{{flagicon|URU}} {{flagicon|CHI}} [[Nelson Acosta]] (2003–2004)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Ramiro Blacut]] (2004–2005)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Ovidio Messa]] (2005)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Erwin Sánchez]] (2006–2009)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Eduardo Villegas]] (2009)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} {{flagicon|ARG}} [[Gustavo Quinteros]] (2010–2012)
*{{flagicon|ESP}} [[Xabier Azkargorta]] (2012–2014)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} ''[[Mauricio Soria]]'' (2014)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} ''[[Néstor Clausen]]'' (2014)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Mauricio Soria]] (2015)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Julio César Baldivieso]] (2015–2016)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} [[Ángel Guillermo Hoyos]]  (2016)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Mauricio Soria]] (2016–2018)
*{{flagicon|VEN}} ''[[César Farías]]'' (2018)
*{{flagicon|VEN}} ''[[Daniel Farías]]'' (2018)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Eduardo Villegas]] (2019)
*{{flagicon|VEN}} [[César Farías]]   (2019–2022)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} {{flagicon|PAR}} ''[[Pablo Escobar (footballer, born 1979)|Pablo Escobar]]'' (2022)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} [[Gustavo Costas]] (2022–2023)
*{{flagicon|BRA}} [[Antônio Carlos Zago]] (2023–2024)
*{{flagicon|BOL|civil}} [[Óscar Villegas]] (2024–present)
{{div col end}}

==Players==
===Current squad===
The following players were called up to the squad for the [[Exhibition game|friendly matches]] against [[Scotland national football team|Scotland]] and [[Algeria national football team|Algeria]], respectively on 6 and 10 June 2026.<ref>{{Cite instagram  |user=laverde_fbf |postid=DY3LZHUjUaG |title=📝 CONVOCATORIA DE LA VERDE 🙌 |date=27 May 2026 |access-date=27 May 2026|language=es}}</ref>
<br />''Caps and goals updated {{As of|2026|6|10|df=|lc=yes}}, after the match against [[Algeria national football team|Algeria]].''

{{nat fs g start}}

{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Bruno Miranda]]|age={{Birth date and age|df=yes|1998|2|10}}|caps=22|goals=3|club=[[S.D. Aucas|Aucas]]|clubnat=ECU|latest=v. {{fb|MEX}}, 25 January 2026}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Lucas Chávez (Bolivian footballer)|Lucas Chávez]]|age={{Birth date and age|df=yes|2003|4|17}}|caps=15|goals=0|club=[[Club Bolívar|Bolívar]]|clubnat=BOL|latest=v. {{fb|MEX}}, 25 January 2026}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[William Álvarez (footballer)|William Álvarez]]|age={{Birth date and age|df=yes|1995|9|15}}|caps=2|goals=0|club=[[C.A. Nacional Potosí|Nacional Potosí]]|clubnat=BOL|latest=v. {{fb|MEX}}, 25 January 2026}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Máximo Mamani]]|age={{Birth date and age|df=yes|2005|4|22}}|caps=2|goals=0|club=[[Club Always Ready|Always Ready]]|clubnat=BOL|latest=v. {{fb|MEX}}, 25 January 2026}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[José Martines]]|age={{Birth date and age|df=yes|2002|9|18}}|caps=4|goals=0|club=[[FC CSKA 1948 Sofia|CSKA 1948]]|clubnat=BUL|latest=v. {{fb|PER}}, 21 December 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Gustavo Peredo]]|age={{Birth date and age|df=yes|2000|4|7}}|caps=1|goals=0|club=[[Club Deportivo Guabirá|Guabirá]]|clubnat=BOL|latest=v. {{fb|PER}}, 21 December 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[John García (footballer)|John García]]|age={{Birth date and age|df=yes|2000|4|13}}|caps=7|goals=0|club=[[Club Bolívar|Bolívar]]|clubnat=BOL|latest=v. {{fb|PER}}, 21 December 2025 <sup>WD</sup>}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Carmelo Algarañaz]]|age={{Birth date and age|df=yes|1996|1|27}}|caps=38|goals=4|club=[[Kalamata F.C.|Kalamata]]|clubnat=GRE|latest=v. {{fb|RUS}}, 14 October 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Henry Vaca]]|age={{birth date and age|df=yes|1998|1|27}}|caps=23|goals=1|club=[[Free agent|''Unattached'']]|clubnat=|latest=v. {{fb|RUS}}, 14 October 2025}}
{{nat fs break}}
<!-- <sup>COV</sup> Withdrew from the squad due to COVID-19.<br />-->
<sup>INJ</sup> Withdrew from the squad due to injury.<br />
<sup>PRE</sup> Preliminary squad / standby.<br />
<!-- <sup>RET</sup> Retired from the national team.<br />-->
<sup>SUS</sup> Withdrew from the squad due to suspension.<br />
<!-- <sup>TRP</sup> Invited as a Training player.<br />-->
<sup>WD</sup> Withdrew from the squad for non-injury related reasons
{{nat fs end}}

==Player records==
{{main|Bolivia national football team records and statistics}}

:{{updated|21 November 2023}}<ref>{{cite web |last=Mamrud|first=Roberto |title=Bolivia: Record International Players |url=https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/bol-recintlp.html |website=RSSSF |access-date=2 February 2023 |archive-date=9 June 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090609165828/http://rsssf.com/miscellaneous/bol-recintlp.html |url-status=live}}</ref>
:''Players in '''bold''' are still active with Bolivia.''

===Most appearances===

|-
|{{flagicon|Saudi Arabia}} [[1992 King Fahd Cup|1992]]
|colspan=9 rowspan=3|''Did not qualify''

==Honours==
===Continental===
* '''[[Copa América|South American Championship / Copa América]]'''
** {{gold1}} '''Champions (1)''': [[1963 South American Championship|1963]]
** {{silver2}} Runners-up (1): [[1997 Copa América|1997]]

===Regional===
* '''[[Football at the Bolivarian Games|Bolivarian Games]]'''
** '''{{gold1}} Gold medal (2):''' 1970, 1977 
** {{silver2}} Silver medal (2): [[Football at the 1938 Bolivarian Games|1938]], 1947–48{{sup|s}}
** {{bronze3}} Bronze medal (2): 1965, 1973{{sup|s}}

===Friendly===
* '''Copa Paz del Chaco<ref>{{Cite web|first=José Luis|last=Pierrend|title=Copa Paz del Chaco|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesp/pazchaco.html|website=[[RSSSF]]|access-date=9 February 2022|archive-date=5 October 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221005074606/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesp/pazchaco.html|url-status=live}}</ref> (4)''': 1957, 1962, 1979, 1993
* '''Copa Mariscal Sucre<ref>{{cite web|title=Copa Mariscal Sucre|website=[[RSSSF]]|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesm/mar-sucre73.html|access-date=10 February 2022|archive-date=30 September 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220930033125/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesm/mar-sucre73.html|url-status=live}}</ref> (1)''': 1973{{sup|s}}

===Summary===
{| class="wikitable" width=30% style="font-size:90%; text-align:center;"
|-

|align=left|[[Copa América|CONMEBOL Copa América]]
|1||1||0||2

|}
;Notes:
* {{sup|s}} Shared titles.
